symptom_text: It's completely broken and won't shine
Nothing happens when I turn it on

ask_questions.q: Is it a rechargeable or battery-operated flashlight? If battery, are the positive and negative ends correct?

solution_steps: Unscrew the tail cap and remove batteries.
Check battery polarity or replace with new ones.
Wipe the tail cap spring and threads to remove oxidation.
If still dead, LED might be burnt out. Needs part replacement.
safety_notes: Do not look directly into the light source while testing.

symptom_text: Light fades after a few seconds
I have to hit it to make it bright
High mode looks like low mode

ask_questions.q: Does the brightness change if you shake or tap the flashlight?

solution_steps: Replace with fully charged batteries to rule out power issues.
Unscrew all sections and check the springs and contacts for dark oxidation spots.
Clean all metal contact surfaces with an alcohol swab.
Tighten all sections securely to ensure good conductivity.
safety_notes: Never use water to clean the internals, use only rubbing alcohol.

symptom_text: Turns on but won't turn off
Have to hold the button to keep it on

ask_questions.q: Can you still hear a crisp clicking sound when you press the switch?

solution_steps: Unscrew the tail cap and inspect the rubber boot for tears or jams.
Use tweezers to pull out the switch assembly, check if the spring is deformed.
If it's just jammed, realign and reinstall.
If there's no click, the mechanical switch is dead. Buy a replacement tail cap switch.
safety_notes: Be careful not to lose small springs when disassembling the switch.

symptom_text: Sometimes it works sometimes it doesn't
Turns off if I move it slightly
Have to press it multiple times
Only works if I slap it
Have to squeeze it hard to stay on
Shaking the head makes it turn on
Touching it makes it go dark
possible_causes: Fatigued spring not touching the battery

ask_questions.q: Does the tail spring look shorter or less bouncy than before?

solution_steps: Check if both the head and tail threads are screwed tight.
Unscrew the tail cap and gently stretch the battery spring with tweezers to increase pressure.
Check visible solder joints for cracks.
If it still fails on impact, the driver board has cold solder joints and needs professional repair.
safety_notes: Stretch the spring gently to avoid breaking it.

symptom_text: Charged all night but still dead
Won't take a charge at all
Stuck on red light never turns green
Tried different cables but won't charge

ask_questions.q: When you plug the cable in, does the port feel loose or wobbly?

solution_steps: Try using a known working cable and wall adapter.
Gently clean dust/lint out of the charging port using a toothpick.
If the port is wobbly or the light acts weird, the port is likely desoldered or the battery is completely dead.
Replacing internal lithium batteries or soldering ports is beyond DIY. Recommend replacing the flashlight or sending it for repair.
safety_notes: Do not force the cable in. Never use metal tools to clean the port to prevent short circuits.

symptom_text: Light is off water is cold
Seems like no electricity is flowing
Bad connection between base and kettle
possible_causes: Burnt out thermostat or heating element

ask_questions.q: Check the metal rings under the kettle and on the base. Are they blackened or dirty?

solution_steps: Verify the wall outlet works, and unplug the kettle base.
Inspect the metal rings on the base and kettle bottom for water stains, dirt, or burn marks.
Wipe the contacts clean with a dry cloth. Ensure they are totally dry.
If it still doesn't power on, the internal thermostat or heating tube is burnt out. Requires replacement of the kettle.
safety_notes: Always unplug the base from the wall before inspecting. Never clean the base while plugged in.

symptom_text: Water is just warm and the light goes out
Not hot enough for tea but it tripped
possible_causes: Thick limescale causing bottom to overheat

ask_questions.q: Look inside the kettle. Is there a thick layer of white limescale on the bottom?

solution_steps: Unplug the kettle and inspect the bottom inside for scale.
Pour in white vinegar or descaler, boil it, and let it sit for 30 minutes.
Scrub the scale away with a soft brush and rinse well. Do not get the bottom contacts wet.
Test again. If it still trips early after descaling, the thermostat is worn out and needs professional repair.
safety_notes: Never immerse the whole kettle in water when washing. Keep the bottom electrical parts completely dry.

symptom_text: Leaves a puddle on the table
Gets wet underneath as soon as I fill it

ask_questions.q: Is the leak coming from the bottom edge, or from the transparent water level window?

solution_steps: Unplug immediately and empty the water.
Dry the base and place it somewhere safe. DO NOT plug it in again to avoid short circuits.
Leaks are usually caused by hardened internal silicone seals or cracked plastics.
DIY disassembly to fix seals often leads to worse leaks and electrical shock hazards. Strongly recommend buying a new kettle.
safety_notes: DANGER: Unplug immediately! Water leaking onto the high-voltage base is a severe electrocution and fire hazard!

fault_category: Indicator on but won't stop boiling
symptom_text: Water boils but it won't shut off
Light went out but water is still boiling
Have to turn it off manually
Rolling boil for ages but switch is stuck
possible_causes: Lid not closed properly so steam escapes

ask_questions.q: Is the lid completely shut and clicked into place while boiling?

solution_steps: Check if the lid is closed tight. If it's open, steam won't travel down the tube to trigger the switch.
Fill water below the MAX line, close the lid firmly, and test again.
If it boils for over 1 minute without turning off, manually unplug it immediately.
This means the steam switch is broken. Continuing to use it is a massive fire risk. It must be repaired professionally or discarded.
safety_notes: You MUST stand by the kettle while testing this and be ready to unplug it manually to prevent a fire.

symptom_text: Forgot to add water and it didn't turn off
Light is on with no water inside
Almost caught fire didn't shut off

ask_questions.q: Are there obvious scorch marks on the bottom or a strong burning smell?

solution_steps: Unplug the power cord immediately.
Do NOT pour cold water into the red-hot empty kettle! It will cause a steam explosion or shatter the kettle. Let it cool naturally.
Failure of the dry-boil protection is a critical safety defect indicating both thermostats have failed.
Absolutely do not use it again. Discard or send for professional repair.
safety_notes: SEVERE WARNING: Do not pour water into a red-hot dry kettle! Using a kettle with failed dry-boil protection is an extreme fire hazard!

symptom_text: White flakes floating in the water
Scale is too thick to wash

ask_questions.q: Is this a brand new kettle, or an old one with a thick white crust inside?

solution_steps: Unplug the kettle and empty any remaining water.
Pour in half a kettle of white vinegar (or citric acid solution) and half water.
Boil it, then let it sit for 30-60 minutes to dissolve the scale.
Pour out the acid water, wipe the inside, and boil clean water 1-2 times to remove the vinegar smell.
If it's a new kettle with a plastic smell, boil water with a few slices of ginger or lemon repeatedly.
safety_notes: Do not drink the descaling solution, and avoid splashing it into your eyes.

symptom_text: Pressing the button yields no coffee or sound
Tried a different outlet, still nothing
Not a single noise from it
possible_causes: Water tank not seated triggering safety cutoff

ask_questions.q: Please check the water tank. Is it full and pushed down firmly into the base?

solution_steps: Verify the wall outlet is providing power.
Remove the water tank and push it back down firmly to ensure it engages the bottom valve.
Check if the drip tray and grounds container are fully inserted (some machines won't start if they are loose).
If everything is seated and it's still dead, the internal fuse or motherboard is likely blown. Requires professional repair.
safety_notes: You do not need to unscrew any casing to check the tanks and trays.

symptom_text: Coffee comes out drop by drop
Pump is loud but no coffee comes out
Takes half a day to drip a cup
possible_causes: Coffee grounds too fine/tamped too hard
Brew head or filter basket clogged with coffee oils

ask_questions.q: If you remove the portafilter (the handle with coffee) and just press the water button, does water flow freely?
ask_questions.yes: Coffee is too fine or tamped too hard
ask_questions.no: Machine pipes or brew head clogged

solution_steps: Remove the portafilter, empty the coffee, and press the brew button to see if water flows normally.
If water flows fine without coffee: Your grind is too fine or you tamped too hard. Adjust grind coarser.
If no water flows even without coffee: The brew head screen is clogged. Use a screwdriver to remove the metal screen and scrub it in warm water.
If it's still slow after cleaning the screen, the internal pipes are clogged with limescale. Run a full descaling cycle with a commercial descaler.
safety_notes: The brew head and water are extremely hot. Let it cool before unscrewing the screen to avoid burns.

symptom_text: Water all over the counter when making coffee
Drip tray overflows and leaks everywhere

ask_questions.q: Is the leak spraying out from the gap where the handle (portafilter) connects to the machine?

solution_steps: Identify the leak source. If leaking from the tank bottom, check for debris caught in the valve.
If water sprays from around the handle during extraction: The silicone group gasket is hardened. Buy a replacement gasket, pry out the old one with an awl, and press the new one in.
If massive amounts of clear water leak from inside the bottom of the machine, an internal hose has popped off or the boiler ruptured.
Internal leaks can cause the chassis to become electrified. Unplug immediately and send for repair.
safety_notes: If you see sparks or smell burning along with the leak, DO NOT touch the machine. Turn off power at the wall circuit breaker.

symptom_text: Ready light is on but coffee is cold
Sour shot due to low temp

ask_questions.q: Did you pull a shot immediately after turning it on, or did you let it preheat for 10-15 minutes?

solution_steps: Even if the green 'ready' light turns on, you should wait 10-15 minutes for the entire boiler and brew head to saturate with heat.
Run a blank shot (hot water only) into your cup to preheat both the brew components and your cup before making coffee.
If coffee is still cold after preheating, thick scale might be insulating the heating element. Run a descaling cycle.
If descaling fails, the thermostat probe is faulty and requires professional replacement.
safety_notes: Be careful of splashing hot water and steam when running blank shots.

symptom_text: Pump making a loud clacking sound, no water
Very loud but not pulling water
Pump is way louder than normal
Tank has water but pump is screaming
possible_causes: Pump ran dry due to empty tank

ask_questions.q: Did the water tank recently run completely dry before this started happening?

solution_steps: Turn off the pump immediately! Letting it run dry will burn it out.
Ensure the water tank is full and seated correctly.
Clear the air lock: Open the steam wand valve, and press the brew (or hot water) button. The steam wand has less resistance, allowing the pump to push the air out.
Once a steady stream of water comes out of the wand and the pump quiets down to a dull hum, close the valve.
If the pump still makes a loud dry rattling noise after a few minutes, it is likely broken and needs replacement.
safety_notes: Have a large cup ready under the steam wand. Hot steam and water will sputter out forcefully as the air purges.

symptom_text: Machine is telling me to descale
Machine is locked out demanding a clean
How to clear the descale alarm
possible_causes: Internal timer reached descale cycle (usually 2-3 months)
User aborted a descale cycle leaving the alarm on

ask_questions.q: Do you have the specific descaling solution or citric acid ready?

solution_steps: Purchase proper espresso machine descaler (vinegar is not recommended as it leaves a smell and degrades seals).
Pour descaler into the water tank and fill with water to the MAX line.
Follow your machine's manual to enter 'Descale Mode' (usually holding a button combination). The machine will pump acid in intervals.
After the acid cycle, the machine will require you to fill the tank with fresh water for a 'Rinse Cycle'.
You MUST let the machine finish both the acid and the rinse cycles completely without unplugging it. If interrupted, the light will never turn off.
safety_notes: The waste water contains chemicals and is hot. Do not drink it, and keep it away from children.

symptom_text: Twisted the timer but no ticking sound

ask_questions.q: If you twist the timer dial, do you hear a mechanical ticking sound like a clock?
ask_questions.yes: Power cord or motor thermal fuse blown

solution_steps: Check if the outlet has power and inspect the cord for visible damage or animal bites.
Turn the timer knob and press a speed button to see if there's any response.
If there's no humming sound and the blades spin freely by hand, the internal thermal fuse inside the motor has likely blown due to overheating.
Replacing a thermal fuse requires dismantling the motor coil and soldering. Not recommended for DIY. Send for repair or replace the fan.
safety_notes: Even if it doesn't spin, the chassis might be live. Do not touch metal grilles with wet hands while plugged in.

symptom_text: Starts slow and takes forever to speed up
Gear 1 doesn't spin unless I push the blade
Highest setting feels like it's off
possible_causes: Too much dust on blades weighing them down

ask_questions.q: With the fan unplugged, spin the blades by hand. Do they spin freely or is there stiff resistance?

solution_steps: Unplug the power cord.
Spin the blades by hand. If they spin smoothly but the fan runs slow when powered, the black square 'starting capacitor' on the back of the motor has degraded.
Remove the plastic rear motor housing to locate the capacitor (usually 1.2uF or 1.5uF).
Cut the old capacitor wires and splice in a new one of the exact same specification using electrical tape.
If the blades are stiff to spin by hand, refer to the noise/stuck guide for oiling the bearings.
safety_notes: Always unplug before wiring. Capacitors hold a charge; short the two wires with an insulated screwdriver before touching them.

symptom_text: Lots of static noise very annoying
Blades seem to be hitting the cage

ask_questions.q: Is the noise a high-pitched metal squeal, or a plastic clicking/clanking sound?

solution_steps: Unplug the fan.
Check and tighten the clips around the wire cage, and ensure the center nut holding the blade is tight.
If it's a high-pitched metal squeal, the motor bearings are completely dry.
Remove the blade and cage to expose the motor shaft. Put a few drops of motor oil or sewing machine oil (NEVER cooking oil) at the base of the shaft and spin it manually to let it seep in.
If it still squeals after oiling, the bearings are permanently worn out and the motor needs replacing.
tools: Motor oil or sewing machine oil
safety_notes: NEVER use cooking oil. It will turn into a sticky glue over time, lock up the motor, and cause a fire.

symptom_text: Pressing the swing button does nothing
Pushed the swing pin and it makes a clicking noise but won't turn
Stuck in place making a loud cracking sound

ask_questions.q: When you turn on the swing, do you hear a loud clicking/cracking from the back, or is it completely silent?
ask_questions.no: Swing motor dead or linkage off

solution_steps: Unplug the fan and use a screwdriver to remove the rear motor cover.

If it's a mechanical push-pull pin: Check if the linkage arm screws fell out. If you hear loud clicking, the internal gears are stripped. Buy a replacement gearbox assembly and swap it out (just a few screws).
If it's an electronic button (synchronous motor): The tiny swing motor is likely burnt. Cut the wires and splice in a matching synchronous motor.
This feature does not affect the main fan; you can decide if it's worth fixing.
safety_notes: The rear of the motor contains high-voltage connections. Always unplug before opening.

symptom_text: Motor hums but blades won't turn
Gets very hot and hums, no rotation
Stuck and won't move just makes noise
Have to push it with my hand to start it
Won't spin just heats up and buzzes

ask_questions.q: With the fan unplugged, can you easily spin the blade with your hand?

solution_steps: UNPLUG IMMEDIATELY! A stalled motor will rapidly overheat and catch fire.
Spin the blade by hand. If it is extremely stiff, remove the cage and clear out any hair or dust tangled around the shaft. Apply motor oil and spin it until it turns freely.
If the blade spins freely by hand but just hums when plugged in (or only starts if you push it), the starting capacitor is 100% dead.
Buy a square capacitor of the exact same capacity (e.g. 1.5uF) and replace the old one.
safety_notes: SEVERE WARNING: If the fan hums but doesn't spin, turn it off immediately! The motor will burn up and smoke within minutes.

symptom_text: Button has no spring to it
Pressing one button pushes another out randomly

ask_questions.q: Are your buttons the mechanical 'clunk' type (piano keys), or electronic buttons that beep when pressed?

solution_steps: Unplug the power cord.
For mechanical piano switches that are stuck: Open the base and spray a tiny amount of contact cleaner or WD-40 into the switch mechanism. Press them rapidly to clear oxidation.
If the plastic mechanism is physically shattered, buy a replacement switch bank and rewire it.
For electronic buttons that are unresponsive: The tactile microswitches on the PCB are dead. This requires desoldering and replacing the tiny switches. If you can't solder, replace the whole PCB.
safety_notes: Do not spray excessive WD-40 to prevent short circuits. Wipe away any excess liquid before reassembly.

symptom_text: Blades look like they are vibrating
Feels like it's going to fall apart
Loud noise and the body shakes
possible_causes: Chipped or cracked blade causing imbalance

ask_questions.q: Look closely at the plastic fan blades. Are there any cracks or missing chips on the edges?

solution_steps: Turn off and unplug immediately.
Remove the front grille and inspect the plastic blades for cracks, chips, or warping. Even a chip the size of a coin will cause violent imbalance at high speeds. The blade MUST be replaced.
If the blade is intact, check the center nut holding it. (Note: These are usually **reverse threaded**, so turn counter-clockwise to tighten). Make sure it's secure.
Check the heavy screws under the base. Ensure the pole is securely attached to the heavy base plate with no wobble.
tools: Hands (no tools needed for plastic blade nuts)
safety_notes: Do not use a fan with a cracked blade! At high RPMs, the blade can shatter and launch shrapnel, causing severe injury!
